risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomized 1:1:1 to receive double-blind treatment with oral delayed-release DMF 240 mg BID or TID or placebo [12]. Randomization was centralized and stratified by site." Comment: The study states that allocation was randomized and gives context clues of a systematic random process (centralized, stratified randomization by site), which is sufficient to support a truly random allocation sequence under the review guidance.

Quote: "Patients were randomized 1:1:1 to receive double-blind treatment with oral delayed-release DMF 240 mg BID or TID or placebo [12]. Randomization was centralized and stratified by site." Comment: The paper explicitly states that randomization was centralized, which is an accepted allocation concealment mechanism because it prevents the person enrolling participants at sites from knowing the upcoming assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomized 1:1:1 to receive double-blind treatment with oral delayed-release DMF 240 mg BID or TID or placebo [12]." and "Each study center used separate examining and treating neurologists who were blinded throughout." Comment: The report explicitly states that treatment was double-blind and that the treating/assessing neurologists were blinded throughout, indicating blinding of participants and key personnel. Because participants and personnel were reported as blinded, performance bias is unlikely overall.

Quote: "Each study center used separate examining and treating neurologists who were blinded throughout. The examining neurologist conducted neurological assessments while the treating neurologist was responsible for all aspects of patient care..." and "All original digital data for all MRI images were transferred from each of the sites to the MRI reading center for blinded evaluation." Comment: Outcome assessors were an independent/blinded clinician assessor for neurologic outcomes and a blinded MRI reading center for imaging outcomes. The reported outcomes here are clinician-assessed neurologic measures and MRI outcomes, with MRI outcomes being objective and centrally blinded; therefore knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have biased outcome measurement.

Quote: "The disposition and baseline characteristics of the overall ITT population (n = 1,234) have been reported previously [12]. The MRI cohort comprised 540 patients ... who had any MRI data: 180, 176, and 184 patients in the placebo, delayed-release DMF BID, and delayed-release DMF TID groups, respectively (Fig. 1)." and "Patient numbers refer to those who provided data both at baseline and at each scheduled MRI analysis." and "MRI lesion count and PBVC endpoint analyses used all pre-switch data and then imputed data after withdrawal or switching to a rescue medication using a constant rate assumption. For MRI volume data, missing data were imputed using the mean of each treatment group at each visit." Comment: The paper states the randomized MRI cohort sizes and that only patients with data at each visit contributed to displayed patient numbers, but the actual per-arm numbers analysed at the 2-year outcome time point are not provided in the supplied text, nor are per-arm withdrawals/reasons after randomization. Because the article does not report enough raw attrition data on the analysed outcome to calculate observed loss or imbalance, attrition bias cannot be confidently judged as low or high from this text alone. The use of imputation is noted, but without reported per-arm missingness at the analysed time point this remains unclear.
